[[RunningHead]]

On Sexuality: Psychoanalytic Observations

Edited by Toksoz B. Karasu and Charles W. Socarides. New York: International Universities Press, 1979, xv + 412 pp., $30.00.

Martin Willick M.D.

Martin Willick

Martin S. Willick, M.D.
Teaneck, New Jersey

Martin Willick  

The topic of sexual functioning in its broadest aspects has been of central concern to psychoanalysts. There has always been an intense study and reexamination of our concepts about sexual conflict and the psychosexual phases of development, but recent trends in research and psychoanalytic investigation have given greater impetus to the emergence of new ideas and further clarification of the place of sexual development in the total functioning of the personality.

The editors of this book, concerned that recent work in the neurobiological and behavioral fields has tended to eliminate conflict theory from its central role in understanding sexual disturbances, felt that there was a "need to present to psychoanalysts, psychiatrists, and other behavioral scientists the latest developments in the field of human sexual behavior from the psychoanalytic point of view" (p. vii). In this volume they have gathered together 17 new papers, each of them providing interesting views on various aspects of our understanding of sexual development. The editors, contributors themselves of three papers, have chosen an excellent representative sample from diverse points of view within psychoanalysis. Like other volumes of collected papers, the book's value could have been enhanced by some brief critical discussions of each of the papers so that the reader could place them into a more specific framework of current interest or controversy. Some attempts could also have been made to organize the papers into sections that dealt with an overall related theme.

There are, for example, four papers dealing with the general themes of sexual and gender identity, although approaching the topic from different vantage points. A paper by Eleanor Galenson and Herman Roiphe on "The Development of Sexual Identity" offers an excellent review of their findings concerning an early genital phase and early castration anxiety. Their observation that a little girl's reaction to the discovery of anatomical differences at about 18 months of life is more profound when the mother-child interaction has been less satisfactory is an important contribution. We need to support more research of this kind, and it would be especially valuable if such children could be followed through the oedipal and latency years. Charles Socarides, in a paper bringing together his work on perversions, puts forward "A Unitary Theory of Sexual Perversions," in which he stresses the importance of pregenital factors, especially in the separation-individuation phase.

[[RunningHead]]

On The Concept Of Primitive Defenses

Martin Willick M.D.

Martin Willick

Martin S. Willick, M.D.
970 Lincoln Place
Teaneck, New Jersey 07666

Martin Willick  

IN CURRENT PSYCHOANALYTIC THEORY AND PRACTICE there seems to be a general acceptance of the idea that there are certain defense mechanisms employed by the ego very early in life, and others that do not come into use until later, when a greater degree of ego organization is present. The former are frequently referred to as "primitive" defenses and are believed to operate primarily before the age of two or three. The latter, often referred to as "higher-level" or "more mature" defenses, are associated with a later stage of development, after object constancy has been achieved and the child has reached the phallic-oedipal phase. This idea is designated as the chronology of defense mechanisms or the developmental hierarchy of defenses.

The term "primitive" defenses is also used to characterize the defenses used by patients who are considered to be psychotic or borderline, or patients who are experiencing severe regressive states during the course of treatment. As a corollary to this view, the terms "higher-level" or "more mature" are used to describe the defenses used by normal individuals or patients with neurotic conflicts who are generally considered to be functioning with a greater degree of ego integration and organization.

There is, in addition, a formulation about the development of psychopathology which links together the two uses of these terms.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Power of Form: A Psychoanalytic Approach to Aesthetic Form [psycholgoical Issues, Monogr. 49]

By Gilbert J. Rose. New York: International Universities Press, 1980, xviii + 234 pp., $22.50.

Harry Trosman M.D.

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Harry Trosman  

This book is an attempt to apply psychoanalytic knowledge to an understanding of aesthetic form. It is the first such attempt in a book-length format undertaken by a clinical psychoanalyst and, as such, is to be commended as much for the courage it displays as for the stimulation of its ideas. Gilbert Rose is fully aware of the difficulty of the task he has set himself. The book is not a definitive study but a beginning approach, and a prompting toward further work in the area of psychoanalytic aesthetics.

Nor is the book merely confined to aesthetic theory. It also offers a number of cogent formulations on the psychology of the creative process, illustrative clinical vignettes, and applications of the central concepts to particular works of arts, such as Samuel Beckett's Molloy, Edvard Munch's The Cry, and William Faulkner's Light in August. Although the style of the book is elusive and evocative rather than precise, readers who take the trouble will find much to enrich their understanding of art in general and to illuminate the meaning of particular works.

In considering the basic assumptions about art in this book, the reader becomes aware that they are very much a product of our own time. The modern view of reality as relativistic and shifting would not have been acceptable to our forefathers, just as the psychoanalytic interest in early object and self-representations, early mother-child ties, and early developmental considerations would have been foreign to the first generation of psychoanalysts, who found the Oedipus complex and its vicissitudes capable of great explanatory power in understanding a work of art.

But reality now tends to be seen as a state of flux, with nodal states of shifting stability. So, too, has the scene of psychological interest shifted to the infant's experience in forming a self and concerns with separation, fusion, and individuation. In fact, it is Rose's contribution to emphasize that in order to appreciate the formal qualities of an artistic work, the "psychoanalytic aesthete"—if the term be not an oxymoron—must have available a ready access to the most primitive levels of his or her own earliest experience. Just as the work of art is consonant with the shifting nature of external reality, reflecting and illuminating aspects of the world hitherto concealed, so the form of the work also relates back to the earliest experiences of reality, as expressed and fantasied by the child.

[[RunningHead]]

Returning to Freud: Clinical Psychoanalysis in the School of Lacan

Selections edited and translated by Stuart Schneiderman. New Haven/London: Yale University Press, 1980, 265 pp., $20.00.

Martin Silverman M.D.

Martin Silverman

Martin A. Silverman, M.D.
Maplewood, New Jersey

Martin Silverman  

Jacques Lacan's influence derives as much from what he does not say as it does from the ideas he has promulgated. His school's popularity in Paris and its environs accrues in part from a fortuitous combination of timely sociological events, but to a large extent it is attributable to his personal magnetism and to the mystical incomprehensibility of the ideas expressed in his lectures (see Silverman, 1981). The latter two factors in combination offer the rare opportunity of embracing an awesomely powerful master whose forceful leadership is welcome but who does not require a rigorous understanding of his ideas. In fact, Lacan permits more or less individual interpretation, according to the tastes and tolerances of each student.

Lacan combines the approach of a surrealist poet and existential philosopher with a keen intellect, an intuitive feel for deeply unconscious desires and fears, and the charismatic appeal of an aggressive leader. He possesses a trenchantly acerbic wit, unbounded audacity, a haughty contempt for authoritative rules and order, a performer's penchant for playful puns and dancing words, and a remarkable capacity for linguistic legerdemain, which he is free to employ without constraint, heedless of the danger of internal inconsistency. Lacan uses his linguistic skills to shroud his ideas in abstruse, mystifying obscurity. At the same time his language sparkles with poetic imagery, melodious assonance, and inspired reverence for words—qualities that delight the cultured French ear and arouse the French spirit. Indeed, Lacan's expression is fashioned much more for the ear than for the eye. He has committed relatively little to paper, and the bulk of his recorded ideas reside in the paradoxically titled Écrits (Lacan, 1966), i.e., in the transcribed, edited notes of his many, often very lengthy, lectures. Only a small portion of the Écrits and, until now, almost none of the writings of Lacan's followers have been translated into English, although a few articles by and about Lacan have appeared in the English language, helping to shed light on the mysterious cult of Lacan.

Foreword

Harold Blum M.D.

Harold Blum

Harold P. Blum, M.D.
23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates, New York 11576

Harold Blum  

THE CONCEPT OF DEFENSE IS PART OF THE CORE of psychoanalysis. As early as 1896, Freud alluded to the psychical process of "defense" or "repression." The use of the term defense was soon temporarily suspended and replaced by repression, which was a specific and central defense mechanism and, at the same time, a rather general synonym for defense. So important was the concept of defense that even during the so-called id phase of psychoanalysis, Freud (1914) had actually declared.

The theory of repression is the corner-stone on which the whole structure of psycho-analysis rests. It is the most essential part of it …

(p. 16). The relation of repression to analytic resistance followed immediately thereafter, in the same paragraph, culminating in the characterization of psychoanalysis in terms of the phenomena of transference and resistance. The concept of defense and the parallel concept of resistance are fundamental to the evolution of psychoanalytic thought and intrinsic to the theory of technique. The importance of defense was elucidated in numerous theoretical papers, derived afresh and clinically illustrated in the case histories and other clinical examples. Gradually distinguished from related formulations of censorship and distortion, defense was differentiated from a global concept to include a number of different defense mechanisms. Freud (1926) then noted the advantages of the old concept of defense,

provided we employ it explicitly as a general designation for all the techniques which the ego makes use of in conflicts which may lead to a neurosis, while we retain the word 'repression' for the special method of defence which the

IN THIS PAPER WE SHALL DESCRIBE A critical period in the therapy of a psychotic child. The treatment was psychoanalytically informed. Psychoanalysis would not have been appropriate as a treatment technique in a child as seriously disturbed as the one we are about to describe. We feel, however, that valuable psychoanalytic insights could be gained by studying the interaction of a psychotic mother-child pair and by describing how treatment helped the child to free himself from a pathological symbiotic relationship with his mother.

This child was being seen in a university-based treatment center for emotionally disturbed children.1 The treatment design employs three modalities: (1) psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy which uses a tripartite2 approach, i.e.

The authors thank the staff of the psychoeducational grant for their helpful comments and support; John B. TO THE END, FREUD'S DEFINITIVE formulations on the psychoanalytic process were clear statements of the indivisibility of quantitative, economic, instinctual drive, id forces and qualitative, structural, ego capacities in considering therapeutic results. In "The Question of Lay Analysis" (1926b) he discussed "the normal causes of neurotic illness." He cited:

an innate strength and unruliness of the instinctual life in the id, which from the outset sets the ego tasks too hard for it. … Such factors must of course acquire an aetiologic importance, in some cases a transcending one. We have invariably to reckon with the instinctual strength of the id; if it is developed to excess, the prospects of our therapy are poor [p. 242].

In "Analysis Terminable and Interminable," Freud (1937) further elaborated this thesis. Here Freud first referred to the "constitutional" or "congenital" strengths of the instincts. Since the inference of presence since birth is contradicted by their strengthening at puberty or, in women, menopause, he modified his terminology to "the strength of the instincts at the time" (p. 224). [[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis of a Patient who Received a Kidney Transplant

Abraham Freedman M.D.

Abraham Freedman

Abraham Freedman, M.D.
1013 Jefferson Building
1015 Chestnut Street
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19107

Abraham Freedman  

SUMMARY

A young man who had glomorulonephritis at age five was treated psychotherapeutically for an anxiety neurosis with phobic and obsessional features. He was referred for psychoanalysis at a time when he was approaching kidney failure, but was referred back for more psychotherapy with the possibility of future psychoanalysis. He underwent renal dialysis and then received a kidney transplant from a brother. The psychoneurosis became worse, and a psychoanalysis was begun about one year after the transplant operation. The clinical report illustrates the developmental effects of the childhood illness, as well as the patient's response to the adult treatment and the renal

954

transplant. Nonanalytic observations by others on renal dialysis and transplant patients are reviewed and compared with the findings in this analysis. The psychoanalysis of a renal transplant patient is feasible if other indications for analysis are present. A previously unreported finding is the problem of changing a lifetime adaptation from illness to relative health. This involved the analysis of the narcissistic conflicts of the oedipal stage of development as well as the preoedipal maternal transference.

The Emergence Of Hostile Aggression And Its Defensive And Adaptive Modifications During The Separation-individuation Process

John Mcdevitt M.D.

John Mcdevitt

John B. Mcdevitt, M.D.
55 East 87th Street
New York, New York 10128

John Mcdevitt  

IN THIS PAPER, I SHALL EXAMINE the emergence of hostile aggression, its precursors, and its adaptive and defensive modifications during the course of the separation-individuation process from five to thirty-six months of age.

Based in part on research supported by NIMH Grant MH-08238, USPHS, and FFRP Grant 069-458, Foundation Fund for Research in Psychiatry: Margaret S. Mahler, Principal Investigator, John B. McDevitt, Co-Principal Investigator. Follow-Up Study supported by The Masters Childrens Center and The Rock Foundation, New York City: Margaret S. Mahler, Consultant, John B. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Biologist of the Mind: Beyond the Psychoanalytic Legend

By Frank J. Sulloway. New York: Basic Books, 1979, 612 pp., $20.00.

J. Masson Ph.D.

J. Masson

J. Moussaieff Masson, Ph.D.
Berkeley, California

J. Masson  

The reviews I have seen of Frank Sulloway's influential book do not seem to tackle his major thesis in terms of the historical material he uses as his sources. Sulloway, for instance, has a chapter entitled "The Reception of Freud's Theories: Myth and Actuality," in which he argues that Freud's own view that he was isolated and rejected is a myth. He enjoins us to turn to the "actual historical record," which will enable us to learn about a "series of misapprehensions on Freud's part" and "Freud's failure to appreciate" aspects of his own work. Sulloway is a historian of science, and clearly the message of his book is that everybody without this historical orientation has not been able to form a just and correct estimate of Freud's place in the history of ideas. One can only know this, Sulloway insists, from an examination of the actual sources, the printed record of the time.

Scholarship, not interpretation, is what Sulloway demands. But does he provide it? In my judgment, he does not, and this is, to me, the great disappointment of the work. The documentation provided is often unreliable. After such pretentious phrases as those I cited above, one would expect a careful series of scholarly observations from the literature. This is precisely what we do not get. For Sulloway relies on others to do this work for him. He cites, for instance, the "systematic researches" of Ellenberger (p. 449). It is worth checking at least one of these statements to see how the printed record and the comments by both Sulloway and Ellenberger compare. For these are high claims that are made on our trust, and we must establish some sort of reason for believing them justified.

We know from his letters to Fliess that Freud (1887-1902) was deeply disappointed by the reviews of Studies on Hysteria(Breuer and Freud, 1893-1895). Sulloway thinks that Freud was mistaken. Thus, he writes: "As for the claim that Studies on Hysteria met with a poor reception … this, too, is a myth, as Henri Ellenberger has convincingly shown" (p. 80). What is being referred to here is a statement by Ellenberger: "The most sobering comment came from Krafft-Ebing, who stated that he had tried the Breuer-Freud method on a few hysterical patients and found that bringing the causal trauma to light did not suffice to cure the symptom" (1970, p. 772). The article by Krafft-Ebing that Ellenberger cites is a five-page article (1896), which has only one paragraph in which Freud and Breuer are mentioned.

[[RunningHead]]

Suppression As A Defense

David Werman M.D.

David Werman

David S. Werman, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry
Box 3812
Duke University Medical Center
Durham, North Carolina 27710

David Werman  

THE MENTAL PHENOMENON WE CALL SUPPRESSION has been relatively neglected in the psychoanalytic literature. Among the major defenses it is unique in being the only one which has been identified as volitional. By that single trait, it immediately throws into question the generally accepted notion that defenses are necessarily unconscious processes. Clinical observation leads one to believe that there is probably a continuum from fully conscious defensive phenomena to unconscious ones. This has been suggested, in regard to suppression and repression, by Brenner (1955). Additionally, suppression merits our attention because, as Laplanche and Pontalis (1973) observed, it remains poorly delineated in theoretical terms. Such confusion is comprehensible given the traditional insistence on the unconscious nature of defenses. Suppression operates in a wide variety of ways: in the daily life of an individual, pathologically and adaptively; an, more specifically, in psychoanalytic treatment, where it may be used as resistence by the analyzand, and as a means for the analyst to maintain an optimal analytic stance. For the moment it will suffice to loosely define suppression as the conscious attempt to "put something out of one's mind."

People have long known that they are often capable of expelling unpleasant thoughts and feelings from awareness. Samuel Johnson advised Boswell to deal with his bouts of "melancholy," by making it

Adoption and Disturbed Narcissism: A Case Illustration of a Latency Boy

Ivan Sherick Ph.D.

Ivan Sherick

Ivan Sherick, Ph.D.
829 West Washington
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48103

Ivan Sherick  

SUMMARY

The analysis of a latency-age boy, adopted at birth, is presented. Clinical material, particularly having to do with family romance fantasies created by the patient to account for why the biological parents "got rid of" him, are focused upon. The relation between being adopted and disturbed narcissism is considered.

responseSet.title: The Therapeutic Experience and its Setting: A Clinical Dialogue: By Robert Langs and Leo Stone.  New York:  Aronson, 1980, xi + 379 pp., $30.00.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Experience and its Setting: A Clinical Dialogue

By Robert Langs and Leo Stone. New York: Aronson, 1980, xi + 379 pp., $30.00.

Stanley Olinick M.D.

Stanley Olinick

Stanley L. Olinick, M.D.
Washington, D.C.

Stanley Olinick  

This is a fascinating but difficult book. The text is a series of transcribed, audiotaped dialogues held during 1976 and 1977 between Robert Langs and Leo Stone. They range over a variety of topics, centering on clinical and methodological issues of the utmost importance to psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. With two such protagonists as Stone and Langs, these dialogues should not be overlooked by advanced students and practitioners. At the same time, I must add that although the statements are often incisive, sometimes even aphoristic, they are also discursive, argumentative, and repetitive. The dialogues have been edited, but they have not been sufficiently pruned and organized for the benefit of the reader. The book, for this reviewer, is one that can be read in only small portions at a time. It is, however, a volume of consequence, despite its polarized or polemic quality.

In greatest part the dialogues have to do with the ground rules of the therapeutic setting—in Langs' term, the "frame." This includes such basics as the use of the couch, the setting of fees, the schedule of and responsibility for appointments, and the "analytic incognito." The discussion progresses through concepts of transference and countertransference. The material summarizes many of the views Stone has expressed in his classic The Psychoanalytic Situation(1961) and in his other writings. Langs presents the adaptational-interactional perspective he has expounded in his many publications.

There is a prompt and clearly defined polarization of viewpoints over the matter of the frame. Langs maintains "that once the analyst does make a modification in technique … the patient wishes for more … to the point of frustration and then rage" (pp. 59-60). The argument leads thence to his assertion that the patient exploits such modifications and that "very often it turns out that there has been some corrupt element in a modification of the frame" (p. 225). This entails a transference-countertransference imbrication, a nontherapeutic misalliance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cognitive Aspects of Splitting and Libidinal Object Constancy

Richard Melito Ph.D.

Richard Melito

Richard Melito, Ph.D.
Framingham Youth Guidance Center
88 Lincoln Street
Framingham, Massachusetts 01701

Richard Melito  

SUMMARY

Features of normal cognitive development during the preoperational period have been described and interrelated with features of the rapprochement subphase of the separation-individuation process and with the beginning of libidinal object constancy. Specifically, aspects of preoperational thought have been shown to be consistent with and to complement the psychoanalytic developmental understanding of the child's renewed fear of object loss during rapprochement, of splitting, and of the "integration" of split object representations. The cognitive processes of centering and decentering have been shown to be central to the phenomena discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Persistence of Very Early Memory Traces in Psychoanalysis, Myth, and Religion

Renato Almansi M.D.

Renato Almansi

Renato J. Almansi, M.D.
139 East 19 Street
New York, New York, 10003

Renato Almansi  

SUMMARY

It is tentatively proposed that fragments of certain early perceptual experiences of the parental objects such as the sight of the face and the breast of the mother, the smiling or angry expression of the face, the pleasing or disagreeable character of the voice, the soft touch or rough handling, the sight of the hands may occasionally be recoverable in the course of analysis. Some mythological figures and religious symbols may also hark back to similar motifs.

The relation of this material to early modes of perception and the beginnings of object relations is examined. The data

417

are correlated with the findings of child observation and of modern experimental psychology.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Relations Between the Negative Oedipus Complex and Aggression in the Male

Daniel Jaffe M.D.

Daniel Jaffe

Daniel S. Jaffe, M.D.
3741 Huntington Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20015

Daniel Jaffe  

SUMMARY

The conflictual problems that accompany the Oedipus complex, in both its positive and negative (normal and inverted) forms, have long been recognized. It has also been well established that normal and pathological identifications derive from bisexual tendencies and from the complications of early life experiences that reinforce love or hate toward father and mother respectively. In the male, two mechanisms that can operate to bring about a predominant feminine identification and hence

981

a negative or inverted Oedipus complex have been described. First, there is that of a primary identification with the mother, and the seeking of the father as object, with castration anxiety as a consequence. Alternatively, the sequence has been considered to involve positive oedipal wishes with mother as object; accompanying castration anxiety based on expectation of punishment; submission in a negative oedipal position as an attempted defense, only serving, however, to reinforce castration anxiety; and ultimately, a reversion to reactive aggression against the father. The links between unconscious homosexuality and paranoia in light of the foregoing dynamics have been indicated.

The influence of preoedipal experience has long been presumed, though not systematically traced. With the advance of understanding of the earliest phases of childhood development, further clarification has become possible. Several factors are discussed in this paper, with illustrative clinical material. Included are the parental roles in perpetuating infantile conflicts of each libidinal phase, and in progression from symbiosis to successful separation-individuation. The resultant difficulties in ego development and in the establishment of mature object relations are described. The subsequent problems in mastering the tasks of the oedipal phase are detailed, showing the effects in terms of alternating positive and negative oedipal strivings. The adolescent-phase struggles are touched on, including the unrealistic ego-ideal structuring based on the negative oedipal stresses.

Finally, the relation between unconscious homosexuality, beating fantasies, and paranoia is discussed, with the implications regarding aggression and its effects on psychopathology.

[[RunningHead]]

The Wolf Man's Last Years

Muriel Gardiner M.D.

Muriel Gardiner

Muriel M. Gardiner, M.D.
Brookdale Farm
Titus Mill Road
Pennington, New Jersey 08534

Muriel Gardiner  

THE WOLF MAN, SERGEI CONSTANTINOVICH PANKEJEFF, died in Vienna on May 7, 1979, at the age of ninety-two.

Until 1977, when the Wolf Man suffered a cardiac collapse in the hall or on the stairway of the apartment building where he lived, and was immediately hospitalized, I received letters from him rather regularly, although less frequently than in earlier years. This may have been because I replied to his letters less promptly, as well as because of the natural diminution of his energy in his eighties.

During the nineteen-seventies I visited Vienna once or occasionally twice a year. I saw the Wolf Man about twice during each visit, for several hours at a time. His conversation was much more personal and intimate than were his letters, which were largely concerned with his physical and mental health. Both in letters and conversation, the Wolf Man was very insistent on remaining anonymous throughout his lifetime, and I and others who knew his identity did all we could to prevent its becoming known. I received many letters, from analysts and others, wishing to meet him, asking his name and address, asking me questions about him or requesting some information from the Wolf Man directly. I was often in a quandary as to what to do.

Adapted from the concluding chapter and postscript of the French edition of The Wolf-Man (Gardiner, 1981).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The New Sex Therapy, Vol. 2: Disorders of Sexual Desire and Other New Concepts and Techniques in Sex Therapy

By Helen Singer Kaplan. New York: Brunner/Mazel, 1979, 237 pp., $17.50.

Austin Silber M.D.

Austin Silber

Austin Silber, M.D.
New York City

Austin Silber  

This is the second in a series of volumes describing what Helen Singer Kaplan calls "the new sex therapy." Her first volume described the "active treatment of sexual dysfunctions." While this volume briefly reviews what is encompassed in the new sex therapy, including the treatment of sexual dysfunctions, it concentrates on a newly defined entity called "disorders of sexual desire."

The author's treatment approach is based on viewing the human sexual response as "composed of three separate but interlocking phases which are each vulnerable to disruption in a specific manner by multiple physical and psychic pathogens, and which produce a variety of disorders which are responsive to specific and rational treatment strategies" (p. 4). These phases are derived from applying Masters and Johnson's description of the sexual response "as a natural biologic function," which was derived from observing "male and female sexual behavior accurately under the same kind of laboratory conditions which serve the study of other biological systems such as digestive and respiratory physiology" (p. 5).

In The New Sex Therapy, disturbances of sexual functioning are viewed as inhibitions affecting specific "circuits" or "wiring" that involve the three phases of the sexual response. These three phases are considered to be physiologically related but discrete, making for distinct disturbances of desire, excitement, and orgasm. According to Kaplan, "Certain kinds of trauma, if sufficiently intense, disturb the entire system, but often only one component is disrupted. One set of causes is likely to 'blow the fuses' of the orgasm circuits, another type of conflict may 'disconnect the erection wires' while a diffuse group of variables is likely to cause interference in the 'libido circuits' of the brain" (p. 6).

This rather simplified, mechanistic view of genital sexual functioning, with its implied foundation in a common-sense and obvious neurophysiological substrate, is presented as an accepted given, readily understandable and thus providing a "scientific" underpinning to Kaplan's therapeutic approach. I have used the author's own words to dramatize her efforts to conceptualize sexual processes in a style replete with mechanical imagery.

[[RunningHead]]

Culture in Context: Selected Writings of Weston Labarre

By Weston LaBarre. Durham, N.C.: Duke University Press, 1980, 338 pp., $21.75.

Waud Kracke Ph.D.

Waud Kracke

Waud H. Kracke, Ph.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Waud Kracke  

Weston LaBarre identifies himself as a psychoanalytically oriented anthropologist. Like Margaret Mead, he is a prominent figure from the old "culture and personality" school of thought and a noted popularizer of anthropological ideas. At the same time, with psychiatric as well as anthropological training, he is widely regarded by anthropologists as a principal exponent of a psychoanalytic approach in anthropology—so much so that he contributed the article on psychoanalytic theory of personality to a major survey of psychological theories (Norbeck et al., 1968). It behooves psychoanalysts, then, to be aware of where LaBarre stands, and how he represents their science to other fields and to a popular audience.

This book, however, is not the best place to go for an intensive acquaintance with LaBarre's ideas and approach. For that, one needs to turn to The Human Animal(1954), his survey of human nature and its evolution, or to The Ghost Dance(1970), his iconoclastic review of human religions and their origin (LaBarre is not modest in the scope of his topics). The present book offers a potpourri of the various themes on which LaBarre has written. All the papers have appeared elsewhere in some form, and only one or two are significant papers in their own right. Most are extensions of, or tropes on, larger works (such as the two just mentioned).

LaBarre writes fluently and with ease. His expressive, whimsical style is often charming, although it often leads him into overstatements and hasty generalizations that are not well-thought-out. His sweeping statements, along with some sloppy editing (several sentences, and even parts of paragraphs, are repeated verbatim in different chapters), are sometimes irritating.

The 16 papers in this volume are highly disparate in style, subject matter, approach, and intent. A provocative introductory essay reviews and defends LaBarre's own prolific work and attacks quantification in human studies. Six more are primarily surveys of some aspect of human behavior, illustrating the variability of such behavior across cultures, or the frequency with which behavior we condemn is accepted in other cultures as conventional or even religious practice. Several others comment on our own culture, offering LaBarre's sometimes insightful, but often rather dated, observations on the 1950s and 1960s (the time of their initial publication).

[[RunningHead]]

A Significant "Dream Within a Dream"

Austin Silber M.D.

Austin Silber

Austin Silber, M.D.
1199 Park Avenue
New York, New York 10028

Austin Silber  

SUMMARY

This brief clinical note is an attempt to clarify Freud's remarks regarding the significance of real occurrences of the "dream

913

within a dream." (1) There is affirmation of the reality of an actual event in the manifest dream (the "tickling" in adolescence). (2) Certain representations regarding real events are alluded to in the manifest dream and are confirmed by the latent dream thoughts (the underlying homosexual theme involving the patient's mother and herself).

Within the ongoing transference neurosis, a new understanding led this patient to experience intense sexual affects which were recalled for the first time during the course of an analytic session. The analyst's attention to this "dream within a dream" led to a facilitating active interpretation of the repressed sexual feelings.

At the same time it was possible to observe a developmental arrest which had interfered with the consolidation of the patient's adolescent maturation begin to be undone by interpretation. The process of disengaging the patient from her unconscious bond with her mother (the undoing of the negative oedipal involvement) had been set in motion.

The "dream within a dream" seems to represent a special defensive effort of the dream work to encapsulate the memory of one or more related actual events and the intense affects associated with them—affects whose pressure for discharge threaten to arouse the sleeper.

The form the dream assumes is related to its hidden sexual origins and engages the active participation of both patient and analyst.

Papers on Psychoanalysis

By Hans W. Loewald. New Haven & London: Yale University Press, 1980, ix + 434 pp., $20.75.

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
Harrison, New York

Mark Kanzer  

This compilation of selected papers, book reviews, and discussions from the writings of Hans Loewald during the past 30 years represents a major chronicle of that age of transition. In it is reflected progress in developmental and structural perspectives, the widening sphere of analytic applications, and always a deepening of historical and cultural awareness. No similar work known to us since Freud's has painstakingly tied together so many facets of analytic theory and practice and grounded them so consistently in the analytic situation—the instincts; the child-mother unity; the differentiation of the personality; perception; memory; the primary and secondary processes; transference and transference neuroses; preoedipal, oedipal, and postoedipal structures and their disorders; language; treatment as psychoanalytic processes in an interactional setting leading to the resumption of growth; etc. Not only does Loewald take positions with respect to such analytic figures as Freud, Jung, Hartmann, Winnicott, Mahler, Arlow, Brenner, Schur, and Kohut, but he situates himself in relation to figures of philosophy as well, especially Nietzsche.

Twenty-four chapters are presented—by no means all of Loewald's important papers. They are divided equally into two sections: "Concepts and Theory" and "The Psychoanalytic Process." However, as Loewald observes, "a sharp and lasting dichotomy between these two emphases is neither intended by me nor, to my mind, possible or fruitful" (p. vii). Themes appear in one article and are continued in another, years later. There is no simple way to follow this material according to chronology or sequence, and I shall be content if I can convey some systematic insight into its approach.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychobiology of Consciousness

Edited by Julian M. Davidson and Richard J. Davidson. New York; Plenum Press, 1980, xvii + 489 pp., $32.50.

Joseph Smith M.D.

Joseph Smith

Joseph H. Smith, M.D.
Bethesda, Maryland

Joseph Smith  

Consciousness, Freud (1923) wrote, is "our one beacon-light" (p. 18) for approaching the understanding of unconscious mental processes and thereby a systematic explanation of mind. If not the one and only beacon light, consciousness must still be taken as one essential bearing in navigating an approach to the study of mind-brain interaction. In the absence of the subject's conscious report, one could not, for example, demonstrate changed "average evoked response" wave shape when the same vertical line is perceived as the letter l rather than the number 1, as E. Roy John describes in this volume. The dependence of neurobiological research on knowledge of conscious subjective experience is not always so obvious and, during the strongly behaviorist era in this country, that dependence went unacknowledged. The editors tell us that "only within the past decade has the subject of consciousness been reestablished as a legitimate area of central concern in the behavioral sciences" (p. 1).

In accord with the title, there is a rigorous effort throughout this volume to coordinate and anchor psychological processes in their neurophysiological substrates. Any advance in such knowledge should be welcome to the psychoanalyst. It is, however, one matter to assume that any such advance further defines the function of the brain and the function of the mind and the interaction between brain and mind. It is quite another matter to assume, as at least some of the contributors to this volume seem to do, that such an advance obviates the difference.

One does not avoid dualism, as Ballenger and I (Smith and Ballenger, 1981) have attempted to show, by simply naming a field of inquiry "psychobiology" or by writing—as Richard Davidson does here—that "all brains exist within a cultural … nexus" (p. 36). We need not, I believe, lament—as does Julian Davidson (pp. 282, 287)—dualism as a temporarily necessary, but unfortunate, handicap to thought. It should be understood that to enter into such inquiry is to commit oneself to a dualistic approach. The question should not be about how to avoid dualism but about the proper way to think the duality (cf. Karl Pribram on p. 61).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Ego Ideals: A Study of Admired Modern Historical and Political Personages

Jacques Szaluta Ph.D.

Jacques Szaluta

Jacques Szaluta, Ph.D.
Department of Humanities United States Marchant Marine Academy
kings Point, New York 110 24

Jacques Szaluta  

SUMMARY

It is appropriate to conclude this study with a statement that characterizes Freud and his search for particular models, and this is: "A genius chooses his family from among heroes."13 His historical heroes were Cromwell, Napoleon, Masséna, Garibaldi, Bismarck, Adler, Lasalle, Lasker, and Wilson. Of course, toward many of these figures, Freud was also ambivalent. Yet these leaders have certain common denominators. In various degrees, they may be characterized as progressive, secular, and

183

anti-Catholic. Moreover, they all had spectacular careers, stood up against great odds, and in many instances had serious conflicts with their fathers or men in authority.

These leaders had a special significance for Freud, and their selection is representative not only of his own personal dynamics, but also of his historical milieu. His ego ideals demonstrate that Freud was ambitious and had partisan political feelings and concerns. In some cases the choice of the ego ideals stemmed from his ambivalent feelings toward his father and his particular resolution of the oedipal situation. Also very significant is the fact of Freud's Jewish heritage and the anti-Semitism he experienced as a citizen of the Hapsburg Empire. Moreover, Freud was always sensitive about the power and the influence of the Catholic church. Still another reason for his selection of widely scattered figures with whom to identify is the broadening influence of his classical education. In addition, being gifted linguistically, he was able to transcend a parochial environment. In general then, Freud's ego ideals reveal that he was informed politically and historically and that he regarded these men as promoting policies that were liberating.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychiatric Clinics of North America, Vol. III: Symposium on Sexuality

Edited by Jon K. Meyer. Philadelphia: Saunders, 1980, 202 pp., $10.00.

Paul Myerson M.D.

Paul Myerson

Paul Myerson, M.D.
Waban, Massachusetts

Paul Myerson  

This collection of papers discusses a wide range of subjects pertinent to the origins of sexual disorders and to their treatment. The symposium makes no claim to present a cohesive and integrated point of view. The editor has selected topics to demonstrate that the field of sex therapy "is in flux, that its identity is evolving and that the unification of 1970 has yielded to diversity in 1980." The subjects of the papers include studies and reviews which are relevant to the role biological factors play in sexual development and in the genesis of sexual disorders. These articles discuss modes of therapy which in essence are physiological or physical in nature. There are also reviews of the psychological assessment of psychosexual functioning, the interrelationship of aggression and sexual behavior, and of sexuality in the aging. These studies appear to be comprehensive and may be valuable for someone interested in the current status of the diagnostic and therapeutic issues that concern sex therapists. Several of the papers present a therapeutic approach in overgeneralized terms; they do not begin to do justice to the complexities and difficulties in treating the sexual disorders under discussion.

Carl Birk, however, presents a quite detailed and by and large balanced view of his way of practicing sex therapy. He views sex therapy as a brief therapy, focused on immediate rather than on remote causes, designed specifically for the treatment of sexual dysfunctions (e.g., impotence, lack of orgasm, and premature ejaculation). He utilizes primarily a behavioral approach, adapted from Masters and Johnson, and Kaplan. He nonetheless finds that frequently a supplementary psychological approach is useful in working through unresolved grief and anger before behavioral techniques are applicable.

Four papers will be of particular interest to psychoanalysts. All four elaborate in various ways on the complex interrelationships of psychosexual development and parent-child interactions as they sometimes contribute to sexual disorders. Jon Meyer discusses the most serious type of disorder—transsexualism. He views this disturbance of gender identity as a "heroic" defense against cataclysmic experience. He suggests that "the pain of a profoundly dysphoric body image and a sense of self can be partially narcotized by the fantasy 'it is no wonder I feel so badly about myself, I am really a girl trapped in a boy's body.

[[RunningHead]]

Homosexuality in Perspective

By William H. Masters and Virginia E. Johnson. Boston: Little, Brown, 1979, 450 pp., $19.95.

George Wiedeman M.D.

George Wiedeman

George H. Wiedeman, M.D.
New York City

George Wiedeman  

In Homosexuality in Perspective, William H. Masters and Virginia E. Johnson present their extensive research on male and female homosexuality, frequently referring to their findings in their previous publications, especially Human Sexual Response(1966) and Human Sexual Inadequacy(1970). Reading these two volumes becomes necessary in order to understand the book being reviewed.

Homosexuality in Perspective consists of two parts: the first, "preclinical," describes the research on sexual arousal, response patterns, and the physiology of the sexual act in homosexuals. The second, "clinical," deals with the treatment of homosexual "dysfunctions" and "dissatisfactions." The preclinical part corresponds to Human Sexual Response(1966) and the clinical, to Human Sexual Inadequacy(1970). The dysfunctional and dissatisfied patients were not included in the research population whose investigation is presented in the preclinical part of the book. Masters and Johnson stress that there was no overlap between the research population and the treatment group. Direct observation of sexual activity and audiovisual recordings were used only for research. The patients in treatment were instructed and encouraged, but their actual activities were never observed.

Masters and Johnson applied to homosexuals essentially the same investigative "laboratory" methods and treatment approaches they originally evolved in research and treatment of heterosexuals. The screening and selection of the research population were rigorous. Only volunteers were used. If the prospective research subject expressed willingness to participate in the program and had "a sense of comfort with his or her sexual orientation" (p. 16), psychosocial histories were taken successively by both members of the research team. This was followed by a detailed medical history, physical examination, and laboratory tests. If the candidate for the research program did not show any "major psychopathology or severe neurosis, and if no physical or metabolic pathology was detected" (p. 16), and if there was "evidence of social stability" (p. 17), he or she was invited to participate in the research. Of the 210 male homosexuals, 94 (or 44.8%) and of the 167 lesbians, 82 (or 49.1%) were accepted. It was a special problem to find young homosexual men, age 21 to 30, who fulfilled the criteria for social stability.

Joseph Coltrera M.D.

Joseph Coltrera

Joseph T. Coltrera, M.D.
Brooklyn Heights, New York

Joseph Coltrera  

Ralph Greenson was a questing heart, sometimes querulous to a fault in the vagaries of his quest, and sometimes more clinically sure of the heart's reasons than of any other. Who else would approach the transference by telling us that it is as much defined by what it is not, as by what it is? "It is our belief that only those patients are analyzable who have the capacity for transference-free relationships as well" (Greenson and Wexler, 1969, p. 381). Throwing his premise into even sharper relief, he goes on to say: "All object relationships consist of different admixtures and blendings of real and transference components … [which] should be separated from one another" (1971, p. 429). Greenson's insistence that transference and nontransference are each the condition of the other derives from a clinician's humanistic faith that the psychoanalytic situation is an open and irreducible empirical system that transcends itself as an epiphany of reciprocating object relationships.

The patient wil be influenced not only by the content of our work but by how we work, the attitude, the manner, the mood, and the atmosphere in which we work… Essentially the humanness of the analyst is expressed in his compassion, concern, and therapeutic intent toward his patient… Humanness is also expressed in the attitude that the patient is to be respected as an individual… Basically … humanness consists of understanding and insight conveyed in an atmosphere of serious work, straightforwardness, compassion, and restraint [1965b, pp. 223-224].

Greenson winced and railed when materialism ran contrary to humanism.

Reconstruction of Preverbal Experiences

Per Anthi

Per Anthi

Per Roar Anthi, Cand. Psychol.
Idrettsvn. 27 b
1400 SKI
Norway

Per Anthi  

SUMMARY

Only a few analysts (Deutsch, 1947), (1952); (Braatfy, 1954) have systematically tried to investigate how nonverbal behavior may be derived from events and experiences from the preverbal phase of development. Their approaches to the analysis of such behavior differ.

Extracts of an analysis are used to illustrate basic technical and theoretical questions with regard to the possibility of making use of nonverbal material to reconstruct preverbal experiences. It is inferred that the analyst's visual observations of the patient's nonverbal behavior may be a cue to significant events and trauma from the preverbal period. Besides, the specific visual imagery of the patient that, in this case, emerged just after the appearance of nonverbal behavior, may be a valuable source of data for analytic reconstruction.

Psychoanalytic Inferences Concerning Children of Divorced Parents

Theodore Cohen M.D. and M. Etezady M.D.

M. Etezady

Theodore B. Cohen, M.D.
M. Hossein Etezady, M.D.
Paoli Psychiatric Associates 224 Paoli Memorial Hospital Medical Building
Paoli, Pennsylvania 19301

M. Etezady  

THE CHAIRMAN OPENED THE MEETING WITH a presentation of statistical data pertaining to families and children involved in divorce action. During the last two decades in the United States, the rate of divorce has more than doubled and the number of children involved in divorce action has tripled. In focusing on divorce and its effect on children, we are in keeping with the eleven-year tradition of these workshop meetings which deal with the cutting edge of the theoretical and observational data pertaining to young, vulnerable children.

Judith S. Wallerstein presented a paper on the effects of parental divorce, continuity, and change at five years. She mentioned her earlier papers which reported on the initial responses of children and adolescents to marital rupture. Those responses were primarily reflective of the child's age and developmental achievements at a time of the acute stress of parental separation and divorce. Findings at eighteen months and five years post-separation also suggested that continuing relationships with both parents, restored caretaking, and diminution of bitterness between the parents, were related to good outcome for the child. This study began with 131 children and adolescents from 60 divorcing families.

From the workshop on "Psychoanalytic Observations among Very Young Children: Divorce." Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association; San Francisco, May, 1980.

Presenters: Judith S. Wallerstein, Joan B. Kelly, Stanley H. Cath; Discussant: Eleanor Galenson; Coordinator: M. Hossein Etezady.

Harold Blum  

CONTEMPORARY VIEWS OF DEFENSE hAVE TAKEN into account the developmental history and hierarchy of defenses; the role of defense in normal as well as pathological development; the influence of defense on other ego functions, and vice versa; change in the means and functions of defense; the relation of defensive style to character and identification; and the complexity of the defense organization in inhibition, gratification, adaptation, etc. Anna Freud (1936) gave us the most specific, elaborate studies of defense with implications for a broad range of considerations that transcended the role of defense in psychological disturbance. Defense was related not only to ego constriction but to ego development, to coordination with, and to adaptation to the social environment and the object world. There is always a price paid for socialization and acculturation in terms of restriction and inhibition, proclivity to neurosis and guilt. But defense also has an essential protetive function related to adaptation and survival, stressed in the antecedent formulation of ego instincts. Defense has intrafamilial and socialcultural dimensions and implications, and Anna Freud (1965, p. 177) pointedly noted,

all defense mechanisms serve simultaneously internal drive restriction and external adaptation, which are merely two sides of the same picture.

Object-coercive Doubting: a Pathological Defensive Response To Maternal Incest

Selma Kramer M.D.

Selma Kramer

Selma Kramer, M.D.
3902 Netherfield Road
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19129

Selma Kramer  

I SHALL REPORT A DEFENSIVE constellation I encountered in patients who had experienced the trauma of prolonged and unremitting handling of the genitals by the mother. I call this treatment of the child by the mother "maternal incest"; I will give my reasons for doing so later in the paper. Here I wish only to call attention to the fact that it was concerning the ubiquitous reports of incestuous experiences with the father that Freud developed the concept of the Oedipus complex and furthered his understanding of defense. I shall not review the evolution of Freud's refinements of the concept of defense and resistance but will instead focus on this particular defense and its vicissitudes.

The early writings of Breuer and Freud (1883-1895) and Freud (1896)

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

ADVANCES IN SELF PSYCHOLOGY. Edited by Arnold Goldberg. New York: International Universities Press, 1980, 562 pp., $32.50.

Most of the contributions collected in this book were presented at a conference on self psychology in Chicago in 1978. The aim is to study advances and to seek coordination with other psychoanalytic theories and allied fields. Thus, the major chapters address self psychology in relation to development, the concept of health, clinical applications, and the sciences of man. At the end, Heinz Kohut reflects on these papers.

In the introduction we find precise statements about the basic issues by Arnold Goldberg, the editor. He reviews the history of Kohut's work and the difficulties in arriving at a definition of the self. As a guide, he poses the statement by Kohut and Ernest Wolf: "the patterns of ambitions, skills, and goals, the tension between them, the program of action they create, and the activities that strive toward the realization of this program are all experienced as continuous in space and time … they are the self, an independent center of initiative, and an independent recipient of impression" (p. 4). Indeed, we see here what Goldberg describes as "the scope of the journey that self psychology has taken in its conceptual expansion," a scope that assigns to the self a central psychological phase, with other psychic functions therefore to be seen as secondary. Later on, Goldberg seems to reduce the meaning when he states that "the self becomes the idea one has of one's relationships with others, with those who continue to sustain and support and satisfy" (p. 5; my italics). He substitutes the usual concept of "representation" with "idea" and refers only to the positive aspects of relationships.

This reviewer sees his task as focusing on the advances to be forwarded by self psychology rather than on a review or critique of its basic propositions.

[[RunningHead]]

Masochistic Pathology and Feminine Development

Isidor Bernstein M.D.

Isidor Bernstein

Isidor Bernstein, M.D.
12 Welwyn Road
Greet Neck, New York 11021

Isidor Bernstein  

SUMMARY

I suggest a number of factors which, in the course of feminine development, predispose to erotogenic or moral masochism. These include: (1) early identification with a mother who has a negative or depreciatory attitude toward her body, genitals, and femininity; (2) the narcissistic injury generated by the discovery of genital differences from the male with the concomitant fantasy that the female is deficient in that respect; (3) the anatomical closeness of the genital and rectal-anal area contributing to confusion, uncertainty, and displacement of attitudes about anality to the female genitals, with consequent devaluation of the female genitals and the sensations and pleasure derived therefrom; (4) greater parental and societal controlling attitudes, excessive frustration and interference with self-assertive and autonomous behavior of females (this would be especially significant during the separation-individuation process); (5) inner-directedness with special reference to aggression and sadism.

The objection may be raised that all I have said restates earlier positions regarding the development of masochistic tendencies in women and is based on the ubiquitous unconscious myth and concept of women as inferior, castrated males. It may further be claimed that my suggestions are based on clinical material derived from the analyses of neurotic and therefore not normal women. It is true that we derive much, but not all, of our clinical understanding of what constitutes the normal personality and its development from a study of the failures of normal development, but it does help us to recognize lesser degrees of those elements in the "normal" personality, to define the continuum between normality and pathology and the more or less adaptive outcomes of feminine development. As

483

I indicated, it was not my purpose to demonstrate the equivalency of femininity and masochism. Sadomasochistic trends exist in both sexes and need to be dealt with in male as well as female development. My aim was to examine those genetic and developmental factors that would foster such masochistic tendencies, patterns, and character formation.

It is through the cumulative experience of analyzing women with masochistic patterns or character structure that analysts have identified the many pitfalls and obstacles that can detour the little girl from the road to true femininity.

In addition to delineating the factors that lead to masochistic patterns, we might try to elucidate those that would have avoided such a result. For example, a girl may have a fantasy that she is a castrated male but increasing cognitive capacities, improved reality testing, and dominance of secondary-process thinking will enable her to diminish the influence of that fantasy. This will be made possible if her superego is not too severe and her own and her parents' expectations of her and themselves (ego ideals) not set unrealistically high. Gratifying experiences and relationships will reinforce her self-esteem and the earlier self-representation will yield to a new and improved view of herself. Similar consideration could be given to those beneficial influences in resolution of oedipal and other conflicts that would minimize the chances of a masochistic outcome.

The present view, then, is that the little girl has some separate lines of development from those of the boy beginning with birth (and perhaps even earlier). The early feminine trend would be reinforced by both parents as well as newer cultural attitudes transmitted via the parents and later through peers, teachers, media, etc. This will lead to a view of herself as different, in many respects equal, if not the same, and in some capacities inferior or superior to the little boy depending on how one measures or values different attributes. Stable, positively valued self-representations will enable her to withstand whatever narcissistic injuries she experiences during the course of her development. Each phase provides an opportunity for

484

expansion of her attributes, including those related to femininity. Satisfaction and recognition lead to increasing autonomy and reinforcement of self-esteem. Sadistic and masochistic urges need to be dealt with by both sexes. This is accomplished by deinstinctualization, by diversion from original aims and objects in a form acceptable to superego and society. For example, the girl may ultimately channel sadistic urges into occupations or interests around caring and nurturing. In family life this would be in the rearing and disciplining of children; in professional work this could be in aspects of nursing, medicine, surgery, or teaching. Masochistic urges may find partial expression in service to others, self-discipline and self-criticism, and endurance of pain, suffering, or hardship where they are realistically required.

I have attempted to show how, by abandoning older mistaken concepts and applying the recent advances in understanding of feminine development, we are better able to recognize and treat the pathological masochistic outcomes in women when they come for analysis. I have also described how the empathic, nurturing influence of the mother and a positive view of femininity by parents and society will facilitate ultimate development of a woman capable of a warm heterosexual relationship and gratifying relations with her children, who is a creative contributor to society—the antithesis of the enslaved sufferer and the actualization of true femininity.

Reanalysis of Child Analytic Patients

Samuel Ritvo M.D. and Arthur Rosenbaum M.D.

Arthur Rosenbaum

Samuel Ritvo, M.D.
Arthur L. Rosenbaum, M.D.
2680 Fairmount Boulevard
Cleveland Heights, Ohio 44106

Arthur Rosenbaum  

THE PANELISTS AGREED, prior to the presentations, that the title of the panel should be: "Reanalysis in Adulthood of People Analyzed as Children."

Samuel Ritvo spoke of the novelty of the topic. To his knowledge, the only other occasion a similar subject was discussed was at the International Psycho-Analytical Congress in Amsterdam in 1965. At that meeting, Ritvo discussed the case of Frankie, whom he had treated as a young adult and who had been analyzed as a child by Berta Bornstein.

He introduced the panelists: Sylvia Brody would discuss the analysis of a seven-year-old child that had been reported in her book on Passivity; Robert L. Tyson, would present his thoughts about the analysis of the same patient, at age twenty-eight; Malkah Notman would talk about the analysis of a young woman in her twenties who had been treated as a child, beginning in her ninth year; Arthur Rosenbaum would discuss the analysis of a young man in his twenties whom he had previously treated between the ages of twelve to seventeen.

Ritvo divided the questions raised by this kind of material into categories, acknowledging that none could be considered in isolation without the subjective elements each psychoanalyst brings to the analytic process.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December 20, 1981.

Panelists: Sylvia Brody, Malkah Notman, Robert L. Tyson.

[[RunningHead]]

Empathic Understanding: A Review of the Concept and Some Theoretical Considerations

Michael Basch M.D.

Michael Basch

Michael Franz Basch, M.D.
55 East Washington Street
Chicago, Illinois 60602

Michael Basch  

SUMMARY

A review of the psychoanalytic literature shows that empathy has always played an important part in the practice of psychoanalysis, but that as a concept it has not been well understood. This confusion and a certain mistrust of empathy that it engenders were traced to an incomplete understanding of the meaning of the term, and to the lack of an affect theory that could explain the communication involved in the empathic process. These problems were examined and suggestions for their resolution were made. A line of affective development that culminated in empathic understanding was proposed. An operational approach to empathy was described and the role of empathy as the basis for and the prelude to psychoanalytic interpretation was discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Time Experience and Transference

James Morris M.D.

James Morris

James Morris, M.D.
1020 Prospect Street
Suite 307
La Jolla, California 92037

James Morris  

SUMMARY

This paper has examined the historical developments in the understanding of the experience of time in normal and pathological development, with an emphasis on the differences between "objective" time, "subjective" time, the "timelessness" of the system Ucs., and "intrapsychic" time. Using a variety of perspectives—psychobiology, cognitive development, psychosexual development, separation-individuation, and object-relations theory—time experience has been seen to unfold as a result of the constant interrelation of physiological maturation and psychosexual development, with early object relations playing a supraordinate role in organizing the experience of time from its roots in identification with the preoedipal mother (Mother Time), where time is felt as intensity, to the relatively autonomous ego-superego function of time experience in the adult with adequate separation-individuation and capacity for object constancy, where time is felt as duration and continuity. Left unanswered are (1) philosophical questions about the phenomenological observation that all time is experienced in the present in the ego, (2) what the mechanisms for intrasystemic differentiation of past, present, and future are, and (3) metapsychological questions about the locus and mechanism for monitoring the passage of experienced time.

The clinical application of concepts of time experience was demonstrated through presentation of aspects of the treatment of a patient for whom time and its experience was the organizing schema for her life, character pathology, and transference

673

neurosis. The fantasy of the omnipotent control of time dominated the analysis and served as a major resistance, yet her manipulation of time served to stabilize the treatment relationship so that analysis became possible.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Self Psychology: A Metapsychological Essay with a Clinical Illustration

Nikolaas Treurniet M.D.

Nikolaas Treurniet

Nikolaas Treurniet, M.D.
Eemnesserweg 24
1961 HG Blaricum (N.H.)
The Netherlands

Nikolaas Treurniet  

SUMMARY

This paper is an attempt to evaluate critically some theoretical and clinical consequences of the psychoanalytic psychology of the self in its broad, supraordinate position. From this either-or position, advocated by Kohut and his followers, self psychology corrodes some of the most central explanatory concepts of psychoanalysis—conflict, transference, and resistance. In an extensive case illustration, I have tried to show the conceptual and technical impoverishment of the self-psychological views with respect to the concepts of organization in conflict, in defense, and in development, and with respect to the role of aggression and to the concepts of transference and resistance. These consequences of the supraordinate self-psychological viewpoint are related to its overt attack on metapsychology, which is linked to an epistemological fallacy as a consequence of the exclusive use of empathy and introspection with grave consequences for our explanatory power, i.e., a fundamental confusion between the realms of content and of function. This also implies an obstacle in the systematic study of self-deception and a threat to psychoanalysis defined as the study of human behavior considered from the viewpoint of conflict. Finally, an attempt is made to integrate the views of Kohut with those of Winnicott and of classical metapsychology.

Riva Novey M.D.

Riva Novey

Riva Novey, M.D.
3700 North Charles Street (# 103)
Baltimore, Maryland 21218

Riva Novey  

SUMMARY

I have traced the theories of Otto Rank as they appeared in his major technical writings. Against this background, I have discussed references to Rank in past and contemporary psychoanalytic literature. This paper describes three important contributions of Rank—his birth trauma theory, leading to his theory of the birth of the self; his emphasis on present experience (forerunner of the current "here-and-now" theory); and his writings about the creative potential of the termination process.

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst and the Patient's Object World: Notes on an Aspect of Countertransference

Theodore Jacobs M.D.

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore J. Jacobs, M.D.
17 East 96th Street
New York, New York 10028

Theodore Jacobs  

SUMMARY

I have discussed an aspect of the problem of countertransference that has received little comment—the relation of the analyst to objects in his patient's world. Emotional reactions stimulated in the analyst by his perception of such objects can have a profound effect on the course and outcome of his analytic work. Such responses are a product of complex interactions between the impulses, affects, fantasies, and defenses evoked in the therapist by the mental representations he has formed of these objects. Such objects, in fact, can have a variety of meanings for the analyst. Not only are they related to self- and object representations past and present, but they may, in his imagination, be part of a network of interactions involving the patient, his family, and other of his objects as the result of the reawakening in the analyst of fantasies, memories, and expectations derived from his sibling and family relations. Emotional responses aroused in the therapist by the patient may also be displaced onto objects in the patient's world and not be recognized as countertransference phenomena.

Finally, I have commented on the way that reconstructions can be influenced by the analyst's perceptions of his patients' objects, and I have made some note of the special situation when an object in the patient's life is also known to the analyst. While

641

awareness of his conscious attitudes and feelings toward such objects can serve a useful function for the analyst, too complacent an attitude regarding the protective value of such self-awareness may make difficult his recognition of the link between the image of the object known to both patient and analyst and the reawakened self- and object images of the analyst's childhood—a link that, in fact, constitutes the deepest source of countertransference difficulties.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Resistance And Character Theory: a Reconsideration Of The Concept Of Character Resistance

Dale Boesky M.D.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky, M.D.
755 West Big Beaver Road (Suite 510)
Troy, Michigan 48084

Dale Boesky  

THE CONCEPT OF CHARACTER RESISTANCE suffers from ambiguities in character theory. I shall argue that the term "character resistance" is a theoretical anachronism reflecting the fact that the concept of character as prevalently applied in psychoanalytic theory is nonphenomenal in connotation. I shall illustrate that the clinical phenomena designated by this term are better understood by utilizing the concepts of multiple function and compromise formation in the context of the tripartite structural model to identify and detail phenomena better categorized as character traits. I shall argue for the advantage of preserving the concept of discrete, descriptively concrete character traits. Failure to recognize the conceptual incompatibility of character as a supraordinate concept of the mind with modern structural concepts has important consequences for psychoanalytic technique and for our understanding of resistance. Such failure leads to incorrect descriptions of how the modern psychoanalyt usually deals with resistance, and it has important implications for psychoanalytic nosology.

[[RunningHead]]

A Note on Consummation and Termination

Victor Calef M.D. and Edward Weinshel M.D.

Edward Weinshel

Victor Calef, M.D.
2618 Jackson Street
San Francisco, California 94115
Edward M. Weinshel, M.D.
2625 Scott Street
San Francisco, California 94123

Edward Weinshel  

SUMMARY

The sensation sometimes expressed by analytic patients, most notably during termination of having left some "unfinished

649

business" (to which they hope to return) is not necessarily simply a judgment about the analysis; frequently it is an affective component of the wish for consummation which has not been granted by the analysis. Simultaneously, it expresses the defense against that very consummation. The wish to give the analyst a gift is in some sense the direct opposite, or more correctly, expresses the defense more openly as a bribe and warning to the analyst that he should not expect or hope for consummation of the instinctual wishes which have been the center of analytic work; i.e., it is a defense against the fulfillment of those wishes almost as if the analyst, by attempting to analyze them, insists upon their enactment. Nevertheless, and despite the apparent contradiction, both affects, which serve similar functions, may appear simultaneously.

[[RunningHead]]

Working Through And Resistance To Change: Insight And The Action System

Arthur Valenstein M.D.

Arthur Valenstein

Arthur F. Valenstein, M.D.
140 Foster Street
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02138

Arthur Valenstein  

SOME YEARS AGO, AN EMINENT PSYCHOANALYST told me about a most intelligent, cooperative man whom he once had in analysis in Vienna. The analysis seemed to progress, a great deal was learned; the patient accepted the interpretations as genuine, and they seemed to be included into the analytic work, to the enhancement of the process. This went on for several years, but apparently nothing changed. Finally the patient himself complained about it. He said he had been coming faithfully for some time; a great deal seemed to have become clear and understandable; it was all very interesting, but he had not changed in any significant way.

His analyst at that point said, "What about your insights?" to which the patient replied, "Oh well, I have them-I have them alright." The analyst then asked, "Well, what about them, what do you do with them?" The patient replied, "I have them. They are on the top shelf of the closet. Someday I might need them.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East.A slightly different version of this paper was presented to the Chicago Psychoanalytic Society and the Institute for Psychoanalysis on the occasion of their 50th anniversary program, November 7, 1981, and to the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society, April 15, 1982.353

[[RunningHead]]

The Inconstant "Principle of Constancy"

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
16 Sunnyridge Road
Harrison, New York 10528

Mark Kanzer  

SUMMARY

A review of the principle of constancy, as it appeared in Freud's writings, shows that it was inspired by his clinical observations,

863

first with Breuer in the field of cathartic therapy and then through experiences in the early usage of psychoanalysis. The recognition that memories repressed in the unconscious created increasing tension, and that this was relieved with dischargelike phenomena when the unconscious was made conscious, was the basis for his claim to originality in this area.

The two principles of "neuronic inertia" Freud expounded in the Project(1895), are found to offer the key to the ambiguous definition of the principle of constancy he was to offer in later years. The "original" principle, which sought the complete discharge of energy (or elimination of stimuli), became the forerunner of the death drive; the "extended" principle achieved balances that were relatively constant, but succumbed in the end to complete discharge. This was the predecessor of the life drives.

The relation between the constancy and pleasure-unpleasure principles was maintained for twenty-five years largely on an empirical basis which invoked the concept of psychophysical parallelism between "quantity" and "quality." As the links between the two principles were weakened by clinical experiences attendant upon the growth of ego psychology, a revision of the principle of constancy was suggested, and it was renamed the Nirvana principle. Actually it was shifted from alignment with the "extended" principle of inertia to the original, so that "constancy" was incongruously identified with self-extinction. The former basis for the constancy principle, the extended principle of inertia, became identified with Eros. Only a few commentators seem aware of this radical transformation, which has been overlooked in the Standard Edition of Freud's writings.

Physiological biases in the history and conception of the principle of constancy are noted in the Standard Edition.

The historical antecedents of the principle of constancy, especially in relation to the teachings and influence of J. F. Herbart (1776-1841), do much to bridge the gap between psychological and neurophysiological aspects of Freud's ideas about constancy and its associated doctrine, psychic determinism.

Freud's later teachings about the Nirvana principle and

864

Eros suggest a continuum of "constancies" embodied in the structural and functional development of the mental apparatus as it evolves from primal unity with the environment (e.g., the mother-child unit) and differentiates in patterns that organize the inner and outer worlds in relation to each other.
